title: CBA the first casualty of Chalmers’ budget
full_text:
Commonwealth Bank has become the first corporate casualty of Jim Chalmers’ budget, as the banking giant reveals a dramatic shift in the mood sweeping Australia, as well as a recalibration as financial markets digest the landmark tax changes around investing. Through a softer-than-expected quarterly profit update, there was a distinct shift in the outlook to the economy from chief executive Matt Comyn towards a much more cautious tone. A combination of back-to-back interest rate rises and Middle East-linked energy inflation is taking its toll and has caused the bank to move from a footing of being optimistic about prospects for Australia a little over three months ago, to now warn about uncertainty and elevated risks.
While the slowing of earnings momentum was the trigger to start selling the highly priced CBA, there was additional force driving the bank’s dramatic 10 per cent-plus drop – the steepest slide on record.
Look to the Treasurer’s budget on Tuesday night with its signature overhaul of the capital gains tax discount (which moves to an indexing regime) and scrapping of negative gearing as squarely aimed at forcing a cohort of new investors out of the established housing market.
Consider that CBA’s share price move on Wednesday was bigger than anything seen during the global financial crisis or the Covid-19 pandemic; the fall also had an outsized impact on the broader sharemarket, dragging it down with it.
CBA is head and shoulders above other banks in terms of market share and flow of investor property loans. CBA has a 26 per cent share of the market and has been growing at about 10 per cent annually, according to brokerage Jarden.
Westpac, which dominated the market pre-Covid-19, now has a share of about 20 per cent. Both ANZ and NAB are clustered at 15 per cent. Macquarie grew its investor housing book at a blistering pace of 30 per cent over the past year, but that was from a smaller base.
Chalmers has sold the idea of his reforms as fixing an intergenerational wealth inequity by getting more people into the property market. But the negative impact on property and growth assets will be significant – particularly as nearly three quarters of investor property and most of the lending flow is tied up in established houses. The changes are expected to put the brakes on investor borrowing, and may even result in lending books starting to shrink.
For banks, investor loans have been a profit centre, particularly in the past four years. They have been driving the growth in the overall mortgage market, and they generally deliver higher returns. Interest-only loans, which are popular with investors, represent high-profit margins and relatively lower risk as buyers are generally more established and rental income pays the loan.
The budget changes “reduces the after-tax appeal of leveraged established-property investment and should slow investor mortgage growth,” says UBS bank analyst John Storey While construction finance will, in theory, benefit from a shift to new builds, the supply-side squeeze from planning to development on buildings means this will take time, and the overall impact is unknown Shares in Westpac ended down 2.8 per cent on Wednesday, while ANZ was off 1.6 per cent and National Australia Bank 1.5 per cent.
The property changes came while there has been an intense tug of war over CBA going on in markets during the past 18 months.
Passive funds such as big global index funds and surging ETF flows have been crowding into CBA shares, pushing the stock to be priced at an eye-watering premium.
Bank analysts folded one by one and, although CBA is a well-run, highly-profitable bank, none could justify a buy rating – given the modest earnings growth could just support the shares at the sky-high levels.
Going into Tuesday’s budget, CBA was changing hands at $171 per share, or nearly 28 times its forward earnings. This was nearly double the average of NAB and Westpac. CBA’s bank shares peaked at a record $192 mid last year. On Wednesday that was down to $153.67, as fundamental value investors were starting to be proved right. The property changes have been the catalyst for this premium to start unwinding. The average of analysts’ 12-month price target on CBA is $134 a share.
In theory, passive ETFs, which have lower turnover, should benefit over active managers and therefore provide some support.
But the market will need more time to work through the longer impact of the tax changes, including whether income stocks like banks could perversely become a net beneficiary.
CBA’s cash earnings of $2.7bn for the quarter were ordinary, but the softness was in line with rival lenders which have also delivered numbers in recent weeks. CBA’s profit number was down 1 per cent compared with the average of the first and second quarters of this financial year. In other numbers, such as revenue and expense growth, it was an uninspiring update from the banking major.
Like the other big four banks which have posted results in recent weeks, CBA topped up its economic provisioning by $200m to give it a cushion against loans turning sour in the future given the tougher environment. Total provisions come in at $392m for the quarter, with lending losses now rising higher from a record low.
Including CBA’s new funds, the big four banks have so far added a collective $1.2bn to their balance sheets for additional protection.
Current “stressed” home loans of more than 90-days overdue rose modestly, although from a low base. Problematic business loans were up at a slower pace. Personal loans, which are the first to go during a downturn, are running at the highest level since 2019. Many are bracing for more pressure to come around lending losses.
In the prepared third-quarter numbers, Comyn was silent about the budget and other banks are also yet to comment about it. There’s a sign bank bosses are also increasingly cautious about speaking out for fear of retribution from a higher bank levy to help prop up a budget.
Comyn says while the Australian economy continues to demonstrate resilience, pressure is coming. “Supply chain disruptions, higher prices and interest rates are expected to weigh on household spending and business activity,” he says.
As the nation’s biggest bank, CBA has long been regarded as a proxy for the health of the economy. CBA has an unrivalled view into the financials of more than a third of Australia’s households, as well as a large proportion of Australia’s small businesses and corporate customers.
It is the most leveraged to middle Australia’s aspiration of owning property and other investments to get ahead. Thanks to Chalmers’ budget, all that is now under a cloud. johnstone@theaustralian.com.
summary: Commonwealth Bank's share price plummeted over 10% following Treasurer Jim Chalmers' budget, which introduced significant tax changes affecting investor property loans and raised concerns about economic uncertainty.
author: Eric Johnston, Associate
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: CBA's share price fell from $171 to $153.67, marking its steepest decline on record, greater than during the GFC or COVID-19.
The budget's overhaul of capital gains tax and negative gearing is expected to slow investor borrowing and impact property market dynamics.
CBA's quarterly cash earnings were $2.7 billion, down 1% from previous quarters, prompting the bank to increase its economic provisions by $200 million.
